neighborhoods_visited: Centro Storico / Piazza del Duomo / Piazza della Signoria
guide_description: One day covering the dense medieval-Renaissance core of Florence: the cathedral complex, the civic heart of the Signoria, and the world's greatest concentration of Renaissance painting inside the Uffizi. The day ends across the Arno on the Oltrarno hill for the view that explains how the whole city fits together.

days.cluster_tagline: The Renaissance city on foot - cathedral dome, civic square, and the hilltop view that ties it all together
days.neighborhood_description: The medieval street grid of central Florence compressed into less than a square kilometer, with the cathedral and baptistery at the north end, the Palazzo Vecchio and Uffizi at the civic center, and the Ponte Vecchio leading south across the Arno to the Oltrarno hill. Almost every major monument is within a ten-minute walk of every other.

morning.notes: Book the dome climb online through the Opera del Duomo website before you arrive - slots fill days ahead in season and the combined ticket covers the baptistery, Giotto's campanile, the museum, and the crypt. Climb the dome rather than the campanile if you do only one: the internal view of Vasari's Last Judgment fresco at close range from the walkway around the inner drum is something no photograph prepares you for. The ascent is 463 steps with no elevator; the final stretch between the two shells of the dome is narrow enough to feel the engineering.

afternoon.notes: Book timed entry online well in advance - the Uffizi has strict capacity limits and walk-up entry on busy days can mean a two-hour wait on the loggia. On a single visit, anchor your route on Room 10-14 for Botticelli (Birth of Venus and Primavera occupy the same room and are larger than most people expect), then follow the corridor to the Caravaggio rooms toward the exit. The rooftop terrace cafe has a direct view of the Palazzo Vecchio tower and is rarely crowded even when the galleries below are packed.

evening.notes: Cross the Ponte Vecchio and climb through the Oltrarno via the stepped Via di San Salvatore al Monte rather than the bus-choked main road - it adds five minutes but arrives at the piazzale from the quieter eastern side, bypassing the souvenir stalls at the main overlook. The panorama aligns Brunelleschi's dome, the Palazzo Vecchio tower, and the entire Arno bend in a single view. Arrive twenty minutes before sunset and stay for the city lights coming on below.

if_time_permits.notes: Covered by the Opera del Duomo combined ticket and directly beside the cathedral, so there is no reason to skip it if you have the pass. The east doors facing the cathedral - Ghiberti's Gates of Paradise - are the originals, moved inside after restoration; the bronze panels on the exterior are high-quality casts. The 13th-century mosaic ceiling of the Last Judgment is the visual template that Dante is thought to have had in mind when writing the Inferno.

if_time_permits.activity_title: Piazza della Signoria and Palazzo Vecchio
if_time_permits.notes: The L-shaped civic square in front of the Palazzo Vecchio functions as an open-air sculpture museum at no charge. The Loggia dei Lanzi on the south side shelters originals including Cellini's Perseus - standing under it is the closest you can get to major Renaissance bronze without a museum ticket. The copy of Michelangelo's David marking the Palazzo entrance is where the original stood for 369 years before being moved to the Accademia in 1873.
